calendar.title: Calendar
calendar.tagline: Your shop's day-to-day command center. Book appointments, block time, run the waitlist, scan QR check-ins, and keep the whole team on one grid.
calendar.roles: All team members can open the calendar. Who can create, edit, or delete depends on the role permissions set in Team → Roles.

blocks.text: The Calendar is where your team plans and runs the day. Every booking — whether taken by a receptionist, self-served through the client portal, or created from the waitlist — lives on the same grid. It shows one member's day, the whole team at once, the week ahead, or a month at a glance.

You'll use it to: take bookings, reschedule and cancel them, move clients between statuses (scheduled → confirmed → in progress → completed), block off time for lunch or meetings, run the waitlist, and check clients in with a QR code.
What you can do here

items: Book an appointment in a few clicks — click an empty cell in the grid and a pre-filled modal opens with the right member and time.
See every team member's day side-by-side (Day view) or the whole team's week stacked together (Week view).
Filter the grid to just one person, to one service, or to a subset of statuses.
Block off time for lunch, meetings, training, or days off — including recurring blocks.
Keep a waitlist and book from it when a slot opens up.
Check clients in with a QR code — either from the URL in their confirmation message or with your camera.
Sign in to Momentfy at your workspace URL.
Click Calendar in the main sidebar (the first item under the workspace name).
The calendar loads with today's date in your current location.

blocks.title: Direct link
blocks.body: The URL is /dashboard/calendar. You can bookmark it, or send team members a link that opens on a specific date — see Deep links below.

blocks.text: Required access
All active members of the workspace see the Calendar in their sidebar. What they can do inside it depends on role permissions:

items: View only — can read the grid but not create or change anything.
Book & edit — can create, reschedule, and change the status of appointments.
Manage blocked time — needed to add, edit or delete blocked time entries.
Manage waitlist — needed to add entries, notify clients, and book from the waitlist.

blocks.text: The Calendar has five views. Switch between them from the view selector in the top-right of the header.
Day
The default. Each active team member gets their own column. Rows are time slots. Use this to run the reception desk or see who is free at a given moment.
3 Day
Today and the next two days, laid out as day columns. Each column is the whole team stacked together. Useful for planning short-term — who is busy tomorrow, what is open the day after?
Week
Seven columns, one per day. Starts on the day you set in Settings → First day of week (Monday or Sunday). Team is stacked within each column. Good for a weekly plan or filling a gap two days out.
Month
A classic month grid. Each day shows a few appointment dots — click a day to jump straight into the Day view for that date. Best for high-level scheduling and spotting empty days.
Agenda

A clean, list-based view of the upcoming days. Each day lists its appointments in chronological order. This is what the calendar auto-switches to on phones — time-grid views aren't usable on narrow screens.

blocks.title: On mobile
blocks.body: The first time you open the calendar on a screen narrower than 768 px, the view flips to Agenda automatically. You can still switch to Month from the view selector, but Day / 3 Day / Week are deliberately gated because they need a wider grid.

blocks.text: Three controls in the header let you move through time.

items.v: Jumps back to the current date in whatever view you're on.
Steps backward or forward — by one day in Day view, three days in 3 Day, seven days in Week and Agenda, or one month in Month view.
Click the date (or date range) shown in the header. A two-month calendar opens where you can pick any date directly.

blocks.text: Booking-date dots

In the date-picker popover, days that already have at least one booking show a small dot beneath them. This lets you spot your busiest days without having to page through the grid — especially useful when planning a block of time off.

blocks.title: Keyboard-friendly
blocks.body: On desktop you can swipe horizontally on the grid to scroll dates, or use trackpad horizontal scroll — the grid enables touch-pan for both axes.

blocks.text: The time grid is tall — it shows the whole 24-hour day. Two things make sure you never waste time scrolling to find the right spot:

items: Auto-scroll to working hours — when you load the calendar or switch views, the grid is automatically scrolled to start near your team's earliest shift. You land on a useful part of the day instead of midnight.

Scroll to now — the clock button in the header jumps to today and scrolls to the current time. A thin horizontal line on the grid marks the exact moment — hover the grid to also see a second line with the time under the cursor.

blocks.text: When the grid gets busy, filters narrow it down. There are three filter categories, all shown as chips above the grid once active.
Member filter

items: Click the Scheduled Team dropdown in the header.
Tick one or more team members. Ticking nothing (or using Scheduled Team at the top of the list) brings everyone back.
The grid now shows only those members' columns in Day view, and only their appointments in 3 Day / Week / Month / Agenda.

blocks.text: Status filter

items: Click the Filter button in the header to open the Filters modal.
Tap any of the six status chips: Scheduled, Confirmed, In Progress, Completed, Cancelled, No-show.
Selecting more than one shows appointments in any of the chosen statuses.

blocks.text: Service filter

items: In the same Filters modal, open the Services dropdown.
Pick one or more services — only appointments that include any of those services will show.

blocks.text: Active filter chips

Whenever a filter is active, a row of chips appears just below the header. Each chip shows the category (Status, Services) and the currently selected values. Click the × on a chip to remove that category, or Reset Filters to clear everything at once.
There are three ways to start a new appointment. Pick whichever is closest to what you're looking at.

items: Click New Appointment in the header — the generic entry point.
Click an empty cell in the Day view grid — the form opens pre-filled with that member, date, and start time.
Use the three-dot menu on a member's column header and choose New Appointment — the member is pre-selected.

blocks.text: Filling out the form

items: Choose the client. Type a name to search your existing clients, or switch to Walk-in and enter the name and phone manually.
Add services. Each service row has its own assigned member, duration, and price. The service dropdown auto-fills duration, price, and tax from your catalogue.
Add more services with + Add Service if the booking covers more than one — for example a cut and a colour. Total duration and price update automatically.
Check the date. It's pre-filled from where you clicked, but you can pick a different day.
Set the start time (hour + minute dropdowns, 15-minute increments). The end time is calculated from the total service duration.
Optionally type a note in the Notes field — anything the team doing the service should know.
Hit Save. You'll see an Appointment created toast and the booking appears on the grid immediately.

blocks.title: Assigning members per service
blocks.body: For multi-service appointments you can assign a different member to each service — for example, hair with Sarah and nails with Maya at the same visit. Each service shows on the grid in that member's column.

blocks.title: Member availability
blocks.body: The member dropdown only shows team members who are configured in the catalogue to perform that service. If the person you want isn't there, add them to the service under Catalogue → Services.

items: Click the appointment block on the grid (or the appointment row in Month / Agenda view). The Appointment Details panel opens.
Click Edit at the top. The same form you used to create the booking reopens, pre-filled.
Change whatever you need — time, date, member, services, client, notes.
Save. You'll see an Appointment updated toast. The grid refreshes immediately, and if the change affects the client (new time or new service) the notifications set up for your workspace will fire.

blocks.title: Quick time move
blocks.body: To move an appointment to another slot with the same member on the same day, it's often faster to edit and change just the start time — the end time recalculates automatically.

blocks.text: Every appointment has exactly one status. You move it through the lifecycle as the client's visit progresses.

items.v: Just booked. The client hasn't been contacted or confirmed yet.
Client has confirmed they're coming.
Client is in the chair right now.
Service finished. Next step is usually checkout.
Booking cancelled. Frees up the slot on the grid (greyed out by default).
Client didn't turn up. Tracked separately from cancellation so you can follow up.

blocks.text: How to change status

items: From the Appointment Details panel — buttons at the bottom match whatever transitions are valid from the current status.
From the appointment block on the grid — the right-click / long-press / status menu icon shows the same transitions in a compact menu.

blocks.text: Valid transitions

items.v: → Confirmed · Cancelled · No-show
→ In Progress · Cancelled · No-show
→ Completed · Cancelled
Final — no further transitions.

blocks.text: Colour on the grid
By default, appointments are coloured by the service category (Haircuts = violet, Colour = pink, etc). You can change this in Settings → Calendar:

items: Category (default) — colour comes from the service category.
Status — appointments are coloured by their current status, using the palette under Appointment status colours.
Member — each team member gets their own colour.
Custom status colours — override each status's colour if you want, say, Confirmed to be green and Cancelled to be grey.

blocks.text: When the service is done, you can take payment without leaving the Calendar.

items: Open the Appointment Details panel by clicking the appointment block.
Click Checkout. A sales slideover opens with the appointment's services, client and price already loaded.
Adjust quantities, add products if needed, pick the payment method, and complete the sale.
The appointment is marked Completed automatically and the receipt prints (or is sent to the client by WhatsApp / SMS / email based on your notification settings).

blocks.title: Partial payments
blocks.body: If the client pays only part of the amount, the rest is tracked as an outstanding balance on their client profile — you can collect it later from Clients → Balances.

blocks.text: Blocked time is any part of the day where a team member is not available to take bookings: lunch, training, a dentist appointment, an entire day off. Blocked time shows up as a muted, striped block on the grid and prevents online clients from booking the slot.
Adding blocked time

items: Click Add Blocked Time in the header — or pick it from the three-dot menu on a member's column.
Choose the team member the block applies to.
Optionally pick a Type — Lunch, Break, Day off, etc. The type has a colour and a default title.
Fill in a short Title or Reason so the team knows what the block is for. "Dentist", "Staff meeting", "Maintenance" — whatever is helpful.
Pick the date, start time, and end time.
If it repeats, set Repeat to Daily, Weekly, or Monthly, then pick a Repeat until date.
Save. The block appears on the grid for all the dates it covers.

blocks.text: Editing blocked time
Click a blocked-time block on the grid to open its details. From there you can Edit (same form as creation) or Delete.
Deleting a recurring block
Recurring blocks have three delete options:

items.v: Removes only the block on the date you clicked — the rest of the series stays.
Removes this date and every later occurrence — keeps the past ones on the record.
Deletes the entire series from first to last.

blocks.text: Managing block-time types

items: Inside the New / Edit Blocked Time form, click Manage Types.
Add a new type by typing a name and picking a colour, then click Add.
Remove a type with the × button next to its name — existing blocks using that type are not deleted, they just lose the type link.

blocks.text: Hiding blocked time

If you prefer a cleaner calendar and only want to see appointments, turn off Settings → Calendar → Display blocked time. The blocks are still there in the data and still prevent online bookings — they're just hidden on the grid.

The waitlist is a queue of clients who couldn't get the time they wanted. When a slot opens — either because someone cancels, or because a new slot appears — you can notify them, and book them in one tap.
Opening the waitlist
Click Waitlist in the calendar header. A slideover opens with three tabs:

items.v: Active entries — people currently hoping for a slot. Notified entries count here too.
Entries whose preferred time window has passed without being booked.
Entries you successfully turned into appointments.

blocks.text: Each tab shows a count next to its label. You can also change the Sort between Oldest (first added first) and Newest.
Adding an entry

items: Click Add to Waitlist inside the slideover.
Pick the service the client wants.
Pick an existing client or switch to Walk-in and enter name, phone, and email manually.
If they have a preferred time window, tick Preferred time and set a from-hour and to-hour (for example 10:00 to 14:00). Leave it unchecked if any time is fine.
Add a note if the team needs extra context.
Click Add. The entry appears under Waiting.

blocks.text: Notifying and booking

items: Notify — sends a message to the client that a slot is available (uses the notification channel configured in your workspace). The entry moves to the Notified state, still under the Waiting tab.
Book — opens the New Appointment form pre-filled with the waitlist entry's service, client, and preferred time. Save the appointment and the entry is marked Booked automatically.
Cancel — removes the entry from the waitlist entirely.

blocks.text: Every appointment has a QR code embedded in its confirmation message. Clients can show it at reception on arrival, and your team can scan it to open the appointment in one tap — no searching.
Scanning

items: Click the QR icon (Scan QR Code) in the calendar header.
Grant camera access the first time — the browser asks once and remembers the choice for your device.
Point the camera at the client's QR code. When it's in focus, the Appointment Details open automatically.
From there you can update the status to In Progress, edit, or check out.

blocks.title: No camera? Use manual entry.
blocks.body: If the camera can't be used — browser permission denied, or the device has no camera — the scan modal has a manual entry field at the bottom. Type or paste the appointment ID from the confirmation link and press enter.

blocks.title: Older browsers
blocks.body: QR scanning uses the browser's BarcodeDetector API. On older browsers you'll see a QR scanning not supported message — use the manual entry field below, or update the device.

blocks.text: The calendar reads three optional query parameters on load. These are useful for notifications, the client portal, the dashboard widgets, and any automation that needs to send a team member straight to the right place.

items.v: Opens the calendar on that date. Parsed as a local date, so no timezone shift.
Opens the calendar on the date from the ISO timestamp. Example: ?time=2026-04-24T14:30:00
Opens the calendar and immediately opens the Appointment Details for the appointment with that scan ID. This is the URL embedded in the QR codes.

blocks.title: Sending someone to a slot
blocks.body: A link like /dashboard/calendar?date=2026-05-01 lands a team member on May 1st in Day view. Perfect for pinning in your staff chat when discussing a specific day.

blocks.text: A handful of tenant-wide settings change how the calendar behaves. You'll find them all under Settings → Calendar.

items.v: Monday or Sunday. Controls the week grid and the month view layout.
Category (default), Status, or Member. Controls what colour an appointment block takes on the grid.
A custom colour per status, used when colour source is Status (or to override the default status palette).
Toggle whether blocked-time entries appear on the grid. They still block online bookings either way.

blocks.text: Changes here apply to every team member in the workspace — the calendar refreshes automatically next time it's opened.

items: Run the day from Day view. It's built for reception — every member is a column, every click is an action, and you always know who is free.
Plan the week from Week view. It gives you pattern at a glance — gaps to fill, days that are overloaded.
Turn Scheduled into Confirmed as soon as the client confirms — the status change is what drives reminder messages and reports.
Mark No-show rather than Cancelled when the client simply didn't turn up. No-show rates are tracked separately in Reports and are the basis for any no-show fees you set up in Catalogue.
Use blocked time for everything non-bookable. If a member is at a training course, block it — the online portal won't let clients book, and the team sees exactly why the slot is empty.
Keep the waitlist visible. Encourage reception to add every "we're fully booked" caller — the more entries, the easier it is to fill last-minute cancellations.
Customise status colours once. A one-minute setup in Settings → Calendar makes the grid instantly readable — green = confirmed, amber = scheduled, grey = cancelled.

sales.title: Sales & POS
sales.tagline: Your point of sale and money ledger. Ring up services and products, take payment in any form, handle refunds, issue gift cards, and close the cash register at the end of the day.
sales.roles: Needs the Sales permission to record or refund. Managing the cash register and approving refunds above your threshold are separate permissions set under Team → Roles.

blocks.text: Sales is where every transaction lives. Any time money moves — a walk-in pays for a product, an appointment is checked out, a gift card is issued, a refund is processed — there's a sale record behind it. You'll also use it to run the cash register, review the day's takings, and issue credit notes.

items: Ring up services and products in a guided checkout flow.
Take split payments across cash, card, bank transfer, online, installments and more.
Issue, reload, and redeem gift cards.
Create credit notes for future use against a client's balance.
Open and close a cash register session and reconcile the drawer.
Refund or void any sale; partial refunds supported down to the line item.

blocks.text: The default Sales page shows today's numbers at a glance: Total Sales, Refunded, Gross Total, Net Sales, and Payments Collected. Below that, a transaction summary breaks down items sold, the cash-movement summary shows what came in and went out of the drawer, and recent appointments, payments, and memberships sold on that day are listed together.

items: Open Sales from the sidebar — the Daily Summary is the default landing page.
Use the date picker to change the day or switch to a range. Today is the default.
Click any widget to drill into the underlying list (transactions, payments, memberships).

blocks.text: The New Sale button opens a four-step slideover: Items → Checkout → Payment → Done.
1. Items

items: Search by name or scan a barcode to add a service, product, membership, or bundle.
Set quantity on each line.
Assign a team member per line — this drives commissions later.
Pick the client (or leave it as Walk-in).

blocks.text: 2. Checkout

items: Review the cart. Apply per-item or cart-wide discounts from the inline buttons.
Add tips, notes, or a promo code.
Tap Hold to pause the sale — see Held sales below — or Clear cart to start over.
Tap Proceed to payment to continue.

blocks.text: 3. Payment
Pick one or more payment methods, enter the amount received, and complete the sale. More in the next section.
4. Done
The success screen shows the sale number, change due, and options to print or share the receipt, email it, send by WhatsApp, start a new sale, or open the client's profile.

Momentfy supports split payments — a single sale can be settled across multiple methods. Tap any method to add a tender; type the amount if it's less than the balance; the remaining balance stays on screen until the sale is fully paid.

items.v: Enter amount tendered; change is calculated automatically.
For terminal-based or offline card entry. Records the last 4 / reference if provided.
Manual transfer; optionally attach a reference number.
Link-based payment — generates a pay-link the client can use from their phone.
Tabby / Tamara if enabled under Settings → Payment Methods.
Redeem a previously issued credit note against the sale.
Redeem a client's accrued loyalty points if the loyalty add-on is enabled.
Scan or enter the card code; balance is deducted.
Record the portion covered by a payer — healthcare setups only.
Catch-all for any method you've enabled.

blocks.text: Hold a sale to pause the checkout without losing the cart. Common uses: the client steps out to fetch their wallet, or a colleague needs the register for a faster sale.

items: During checkout, tap Hold. Enter an optional label ("Maya — waiting for card") and confirm.
Resume from the Held Sales button in the header — all held carts are listed.
Pick one to continue exactly where you left off, or delete it to discard.

blocks.text: Every sale generates a receipt. The template (A4 Standard or POS thermal) and header/footer text are set under Settings → Receipts. From the Done screen or the transaction detail, you can:

items: Print to a connected printer.
Download as a PDF.
Email to the client (auto-send can be toggled in Settings → Receipts).
Share a WhatsApp message with the receipt link and summary.

blocks.text: Every completed, voided, and refunded sale shows up under Sales → Transactions. Filter by date range, status, and search by sale number or client. Each row has a View Details action opening a read-only breakdown of items, discounts, taxes, tenders, and linked appointment.

blocks.title: Need a receipt from a past sale?
blocks.body: Open any transaction from the list — the same Print / PDF / Email / WhatsApp buttons are available on the detail view.

blocks.text: Two different actions for two different scenarios.

items.v: Money moves back to the client. Can be partial (select specific lines or enter a custom amount) or full. You pick the refund method — usually back to the original tender.
Cancels a sale as if it never happened. No payment is reversed — used for mistakes caught before the client left the counter.

blocks.text: Processing a refund

items: Open the sale from Transactions.
Click Refund. Choose items (for line-by-line) or switch to the Amount tab for a custom refund total.
Pick the refund method and the team member processing it.
Type a reason — it appears in reports and the audit trail.
Confirm. If the sale's commissions were already paid out, a warning shows that the affected team members will see a clawback in their next pay run.

blocks.title: Refund approvals
blocks.body: If the refund amount is above the threshold set under Settings → Approvals, it goes into Accounting → Pending Refunds. A different team member than the one who raised it has to approve or reject before the client actually gets the money back.

blocks.text: Appointments can be checked out straight from the Calendar (see the Calendar docs), but you can also reach them here.

items: Open Sales → Appointments.
Filter the list by date range or status; the default is this month.
Click a completed appointment and tap Checkout — the New Sale slideover opens pre-filled with the appointment's services, client, and price.
Adjust if needed (add a retail product, apply a discount), take payment, and complete.

blocks.text: Gift cards are reloadable balance instruments your clients can buy for themselves or someone else. Each card has a unique code (for example GC-AB12-CD34) and a balance that gets debited as it's used.
Issuing a gift card

items: Open Sales → Gift Cards and click Issue Gift Card.
Enter the amount, recipient name, recipient email (optional — the card is emailed if filled), and the purchaser's name.
Save. A code is generated and the card shows up in the list as Active.

blocks.text: Checking balance & voiding

items: Use Check Balance to look up any card's remaining value by code.
Void Card forfeits the balance — it cannot be redeemed again. Useful when a card is reported lost.

blocks.text: A credit note is a future-use balance attached to a specific client. Common reasons: goodwill, partial refund in store credit, a product returned without immediate cash refund.

items: Open Finance → Credit Notes and click New Credit Note.
Pick the client, amount, reason, and issue date.
Save. The client now has that balance available to redeem at their next checkout.

blocks.title: Using a credit note
blocks.body: At checkout, add Credit Note as a payment method. The system shows the client's available balance and applies it against the sale.

blocks.text: If your location takes cash, the cash register keeps the drawer reconciled. Each day (or shift) you open a session, take cash in and out, and close it against what you actually count in the drawer.

items: Open Sales → Cash Register.
Tap Open Session. Enter the opening float (what's already in the drawer) and confirm.
During the day, use Add Cash Movement to record anything that isn't tied to a sale — bank drop, petty cash, tip-out.
At the end of the day, tap Close Session. Enter the counted amount; the system shows the expected balance and the difference.

blocks.title: One session per location
blocks.body: Cash register is scoped to a location. If you have multiple branches, each opens its own session — the drawers are reconciled independently.

items: Always assign a team member on each line — commissions only attribute to the person on the line, not to whoever pressed the buttons.
Use the Note field on a sale for anything unusual — it surfaces in reports and makes refunds 6 months later much easier to unpick.
Hold sales, don't abandon them. Deleting a cart loses any discount, promo code or note you entered.
Close the register every day. An unclosed session inflates the next day's KPIs and breaks the daily cash report.

clients.title: Clients
clients.tagline: Your customer database. Profiles, contact details, visit history, notes, consent, segments, and marketing campaigns — everything that follows a person across the business.
clients.roles: The Clients permission gates add/edit/deactivate. Blast campaigns and segments are separate permissions for team members who handle marketing.

blocks.text: Clients is your customer database. Every person who walks in, books online, or receives a marketing message has a profile here. Their appointments, sales, payments, invoices, notes, and consent preferences all live under their name so you can see the whole relationship at a glance.
The main page is a search-first, filterable table of everyone in your database.

items: Search by name, phone, or email.
Filter by status (active/inactive) and date added.
Sort by newest, oldest, or name A–Z / Z–A.
Import a CSV to bulk-create clients (duplicate email / phone is skipped with a report).
Export the filtered list as CSV for external tools.
Each row shows name, phone, email, total sales, appointments count, and last visit.
Click Add Client. The profile modal opens with several collapsible sections.
Fill in Basic Info — first name, last name, email, phone, gender, birthday. Only name is strictly required, but email or phone is needed for notifications.
Expand Additional Details if you collect IDs, country, occupation, preferred language, client source, or a referrer.
Open Business Client if this is a B2B account — enter the VAT and CR numbers for ZATCA-compliant standard invoices.
Use Advanced for medical fields (allergies, emergency contacts), pronouns, and multiple saved addresses.
Save. The profile opens immediately so you can continue setting up notes or preferences.

blocks.title: Walk-in at the POS
blocks.body: You don't have to create a client before ringing up a sale — every checkout has a Walk-in toggle. If the person becomes a regular, you can convert the walk-in to a full profile later.

blocks.text: Clicking any client opens a tabbed profile. Each tab is a different angle on the same person.

items.v: Key facts: joined date, total sales, appointment counts, last visit, quick actions.
All contact and demographic fields. Edit Profile re-opens the form used to create the client.
Filterable list of past, upcoming, and cancelled appointments. Book Again is a one-click repeat.
Every transaction linked to this client — open a row for its full receipt.
The payment side of sales: date, method, amount.
Formal invoices issued to the client (ZATCA status shown if simplified/standard e-invoicing is enabled).
Appointment and marketing consent toggles. Also controls booking portal access and password.

blocks.text: Notes are a running log attached to the client. They appear on the Notes tab and can also be pinned to show at the top of the profile overview.

items: Add a note any time — allergies, preferences, off-record reminders.
Pin anything critical so it's the first thing the team sees when the client walks in.
Notes are ordered newest-first by default; pinned notes float to the top.

blocks.text: Standard segments are read-only, automatically populated groupings. Open Clients → Segments → Standard to see who currently falls into each one.

items.v: Added in the last 30 days.
Had at least one appointment in the last 30 days.
Booked but no past history — first-timers.
2 or more sales in the last 5 months.
3 or more sales historically, none in the last 2 months.
At least 500 of local currency in sales over the last 12 months.
Birthday in the next 30 days.

blocks.title: These drive campaigns
blocks.body: Every standard segment can be the audience of a Blast Campaign — a birthday wish, a win-back offer for Lapsed Clients, a VIP discount for High Spenders.

items: Open Clients → Segments → Custom and click Add.
Give the segment a name and description ("Haircut regulars — last 90 days").
Set the rules: minimum sales count, time window, minimum spend, last-visit window, category of service, tags attached, and so on.
Optionally toggle Show as badge so the segment appears as a label on the client profile — useful for VIPs.
Save. The audience count updates in real time as clients match or leave the criteria.

blocks.title: Straight to a campaign
blocks.body: Every custom segment has a Create Blast Campaign shortcut that opens the campaign editor with the segment pre-selected.

blocks.text: Blast campaigns send SMS, WhatsApp, or email to everyone in a segment. The same workflow for a holiday greeting, a promo code, or a feedback request.

items: Open Clients → Campaigns and click New Campaign.
Name the campaign, pick a segment, and choose the channel (SMS, WhatsApp, or email).
For email, add a subject. For any channel, write the message body — use {firstName}, {lastName}, {fullName}, or {businessName} to personalize.
Review the preview: how many recipients there are, how many will be skipped because they haven't given marketing consent.
Click Send now — or schedule if that option is enabled in your plan.

blocks.title: Consent is respected
blocks.body: Clients who opted out of marketing are automatically excluded from blast campaigns, even if they're in the selected segment. The excluded count is always shown in the preview so you can see the real reach.

blocks.text: Each client has two independent consent switches, visible from the profile Notifications tab.

items.v: Reminders, confirmations, cancellation notices. Usually kept on for anyone with upcoming bookings.
Blast campaigns, birthday messages, win-back offers. This is the one clients can opt out of without losing transactional messages.

blocks.text: Deactivation

Deactivating a client keeps their history intact but removes them from the active list, search, and campaign audiences. You can reactivate at any time. For full data deletion — a GDPR right-to-erasure request, for example — use the tools under Settings → Data Retention.

items: Capture phone numbers on every first visit — they're worth more than email for SMS reminders and drive no-show rates down.
Pin important notes (allergies, preferred language, "no small talk") — they should hit the team the moment the client walks in.
Use custom segments before every campaign rather than shooting to all clients — engagement rates double when the audience is narrowed.
Review Lapsed Clients monthly. A small, targeted win-back message usually outperforms a broad promo.

catalogue.title: Catalogue
catalogue.tagline: Everything you sell. Services, products, bundles, memberships, discounts, and smart pricing all live together so the Calendar, Sales, and Online Booking can pull from one source of truth.
catalogue.roles: Edit access is gated by the Catalogue permission. Discounts and Smart Pricing often sit under a Manager role since they affect margin.

blocks.text: The Catalogue is your price list. Anything with a price tag lives here — the services you deliver, the retail products you sell at checkout, and the packages, memberships, and promotions you build on top.

Everything else in the app pulls from here: the Calendar shows services and durations, the POS shows prices and tax, the online booking site shows what clients can buy, and Reports reference categories and price points.

items: Open Catalogue → Services and click Add Service.
Fill in Service Details — name, category (required; groups the service for colour-coding and reports), description, and optional icon.
Set Pricing & Duration — price, duration in minutes, tax (inherit / none / specific rate), and commission type for team members.
Pick Location Availability if you have multiple branches — not every service runs at every location.
Choose Service Mode — in-person, online (with a Zoom link auto-generated), or both.
Assign Team Members who can deliver this service; only they appear in the member dropdown on the Calendar.
Save. The service is immediately available in the Calendar and POS.

blocks.text: Products are everything you sell from the retail counter or online store — shampoos, skincare, branded merchandise. Each product has pricing, inventory levels, and optional variants.

items: Basic Info: name, category, brand, description.
Pricing: cost (supply), retail price, markup / profit, and tax.
Inventory: SKU, current stock, low-stock level, reorder quantity, and supplier (feeds the Inventory module's reorder alerts).
Commission: toggle whether team members earn commission on this product.
Photos: upload multiple images; mark one as the main one for the booking site.
Variants: sizes, colours, flavours — each variant has its own SKU, price, and stock count.
Barcode: auto-generate or type one in; scan-to-add at the POS works immediately.

blocks.text: A bundle is a package of services — often priced at a discount against the individual services. Think "Bridal Package" (makeup + hair + lashes) or "New Year Glow" (facial + mani + pedi).
Pricing modes

items.v: Total is the sum of each service's own price. No bundle discount — useful for grouping only.
A fixed price you set for the whole bundle, regardless of the services inside.
Calculated as "sum of services, minus X percent". Updates automatically if a service price changes.

blocks.text: Delivery

items: Sequence: run services back-to-back in a fixed order, or in parallel (useful if two team members can work simultaneously).
Online booking: toggle whether the bundle appears on the client booking site.
Gender availability: all, male-only, female-only — matches the client's saved gender preference.

blocks.text: Memberships are recurring plans — a flat monthly fee for unlimited services, a quarterly bundle, a pay-once-get-one-free deal. They bind the client to you and feed predictable revenue.

items: Open Catalogue → Memberships and click Add Membership.
Set the billing cycle: weekly, monthly, quarterly, or yearly.
Set the duration: a fixed number of days, or unlimited (rolling subscription).
Set the appointment allowance: a max number of included appointments (e.g. 4 per month) or unlimited.
Set a discount percent that applies to any extra services beyond the allowance.
List the benefits — free text that appears on the booking site and printed contracts.
Save. The membership is now available to sell at the POS and on the booking site.

blocks.text: Discounts cover everything from an auto-applied happy-hour deal to a single-use promo code.

items.v: Automatic (applied whenever conditions match) or Code (requires the client to enter a code).
Percentage off · Fixed amount off · Buy X get Y free.
Entire cart, specific items, or a whole category.
Minimum purchase, minimum item count, restrict to a service/product/category or membership holders.
Start and end dates, max total uses, max per customer.
Exclusive (cannot stack) or stackable with other discounts.
All branches or specific ones.

blocks.text: Smart Pricing lets you flex prices by day and time — peak hours up, off-peak down.

items: Open Catalogue → Smart Pricing and toggle the master switch to On.
Click Add Rule. Give it a name ("Weekend peak", "Monday twilight").
Pick the day of week and the time range.
Choose the direction — increase or decrease — and the adjustment (percentage or fixed amount).
Choose which services it applies to (all or a selection) and which branch.
Save. Rules list in the timeline view, grouped by day; a green Active right now label shows whenever a rule matches.

blocks.title: Quote at the right time
blocks.body: Prices on the booking site and POS always reflect the rule for the slot the service is booked for, not the moment the client is browsing. A Tuesday at 8pm appointment quoted at 6pm will still show the Tuesday-8pm price.

items: Category every service — the Calendar uses service category as the default colour source, and reports group revenue by category.
Keep descriptions short but specific on the booking site. "45-min cut and style with a senior stylist" beats "Haircut".
Use percentage bundles rather than custom bundles where you can — the total re-calculates if you change a service price later.
Test a discount before publicising it. Add it to a cart with Preview to confirm it stacks (or doesn't) the way you expect.

inventory.title: Inventory
inventory.tagline: Physical stock management. Track levels, adjust counts, run stocktakes, transfer between branches, raise purchase orders, and pay suppliers — all from one place.
inventory.roles: The Inventory permission gates every action. Suppliers and payments can be split into a separate role for an ops/purchasing lead.

blocks.text: Product definitions (name, SKU, cost, price) live in the Catalogue. Inventory is where the physical reality of those products — how many are in the drawer right now, how many are in transit from another branch, how many are on order from the supplier — is tracked.
Every time a product is sold at the POS, stock goes down. Every time you receive a stock order or a transfer, stock goes up. The dashboards and reports here tell you the story.
Inventory → Stock is the home screen. The top KPIs summarise the whole book: number of active products, items below reorder level (with a red alert if any), and total stock value at cost.

items: Search by product name or SKU — live as you type.
Filter to Low Stock to see only items below reorder level.
Sort by newest or oldest added.
Scan a barcode with the camera or a USB scanner — jumps straight to that product's adjustment modal.
Export the current view as CSV.
Click a product row (or scan its barcode) to open the Adjust Stock modal.
Type the new quantity — or the delta (+5 / −3) depending on your preference.
Add an optional reason and save. The change is logged as "Manual adjustment from stock page" in the movement history.

blocks.title: Use stocktakes for big corrections
blocks.body: Manual adjustments are fine for a single item. If you're reconciling a whole shelf, run a stocktake (next chapter) — you get a persistent record and a clear expected-vs-counted audit trail.

blocks.text: A stocktake is a physical count checkpoint. You pick a moment, count everything (or a subset), and the system records the difference from what it expected.

items: Open Inventory → Stocktakes and click New Stocktake. Give it a name ("March month-end") and optional notes.
The system snapshots expected quantities. Status is Draft.
Click Start Stocktake. Walk the shelves, scan or search each product, and type the counted quantity.
Filter to Discrepancies only to focus on anything that's off.
Save as you go — you can come back and continue.
When the count is done, Complete. Discrepancies turn into auto-adjustments. Or Cancel to discard everything.

blocks.text: When you have more than one branch, stock often needs to move — extra shampoo from the flagship branch to the new location, seasonal stock pulled back to the main store.

items: Open Inventory → Transfers and click New Transfer.
Pick the source and destination locations.
Add items with quantities — the system validates availability at the source.
Save as Draft, or mark Sent to deduct stock at the source.
At the receiving end, the destination manager clicks Receive to add stock. Partial receipts are supported if something's missing.

items.v: Editable, no stock moved yet.
Source stock deducted, in transit.
Destination stock added, transfer closed.
Some items received; the rest can still be received or cancelled.

blocks.text: Stock orders are purchase orders to suppliers. They track what you asked for, when it was ordered, what actually arrived, and at what cost.

items: Open Inventory → Stock Orders and click New Order.
Pick the supplier. Add products with quantities and unit cost — cost prices pre-fill from the product.
Save as Draft while you iterate, or move to Ordered once the supplier has confirmed.
When stock arrives, open the order and Mark as Received. Partial receipts are fine — the rest stays open until all items arrive.
Stock is added to the destination location on receipt. A bill is optionally generated in Accounting for payment tracking.
Open Inventory → Suppliers and click Add Supplier.
Fill in the basics: supplier name, contact person, email, phone, website, and the full address. Pin the map location if it helps delivery.
Save. You can now link products to this supplier (which drives reorder alerts) and raise stock orders against them.

blocks.title: Bulk import
blocks.body: If you're onboarding from a spreadsheet, use the CSV import — columns for name, email, phone, and address are enough to get started.

blocks.text: Supplier Payments is the A/P side — what you owe your suppliers and what you've paid them. Each payment can settle one or more bills generated by received stock orders (or manually created bills in Accounting).

items: Open Inventory → Supplier Payments and click New Payment.
Pick the supplier, the payment date, and the amount.
Allocate the amount against outstanding bills — the system suggests oldest-first.
Save. The bill status updates (Partially Paid / Paid) and a journal entry is auto-posted.
Set reorder levels on every product. Low-stock alerts are only useful if the thresholds are real.
Do monthly stocktakes on high-turnover items. Quarterly is enough for slow movers.
Always receive stock orders through the system — even for a walk-in purchase from a cash supplier — otherwise your stock value slowly drifts from reality.
Use transfers, not manual adjustments, for moving stock between branches. Otherwise the inter-branch flow disappears from the reports.

team.title: Team
team.tagline: Your staff. Members, schedules, permissions, commissions, attendance, payroll, and leave — the HR operating system of your booking business.
team.roles: Basic team visibility is open. Inviting new members, setting permissions, processing payroll, and approving leave are gated by separate permissions so a shift lead can read without being able to pay anyone.

blocks.text: Team is the one place where everything about your staff comes together — who they are, when they work, what they can do in the app, what they earn, and how their hours and leave get logged. The Calendar pulls their schedule and blocked time. Sales pulls their commission rates. Accounting pulls their payroll.
The Members tab lists everyone on your team — active and inactive — with their role, calendar colour, and location assignment.

items: Search by name or email.
Filter by status (active / inactive) or location.
Open any member for the full profile (Profile, Finance, Schedule, Permissions, Notifications tabs).
Deactivate a member to hide them from the Calendar and the booking site without deleting their history.
Remove a member for good — destructive, only the owner role can do this and only non-owner members can be removed.
Click Add Team Member on the Members tab.
Choose a role (or "Custom" to configure permissions manually).
Generate an invite link. The link is valid for 7 days.
Share the link — email, WhatsApp, SMS, paper if you must.
The invitee opens the link, creates a password, and lands in the dashboard as a full member.

blocks.title: Role presets
blocks.body: If you find yourself setting up the same permissions again and again, save them as a Permission Template under Settings → Permission Templates and apply the template on invite.

blocks.text: Each member has a weekly schedule — the days and hours they're available. The Calendar reads this to show shifts on the grid and to reject online bookings outside their hours.

items: Open the member profile and go to the Schedule tab.
For each day Monday–Sunday, either mark it as Day Off or add one or more shifts.
Within any shift, add breaks — the Calendar paints these as labelled blocks so clients can't be booked into lunch.
Save. Changes apply immediately to the Calendar and online booking.

blocks.title: Copy schedules
blocks.body: If ten stylists all work the same Monday–Saturday rhythm, set one up perfectly and use Copy schedule from one member to the rest — you only need to tweak the exceptions.

blocks.text: Permissions are granular toggles per feature. Each member gets either full access or a custom set: Appointments, Clients, Catalogue, Sales, Team, Reports, Settings, Inventory, Booking, Addons, Tickets, Resources, Events, Expenses, Credit Notes, Cash Register, Follow-ups, Loyalty, Insurance.

Notifications decide what they're told about. Each member ticks which events trigger a notification for them — new online bookings, appointment no-shows, low-stock alerts, stock-order arrivals, daily summaries, ticket replies. Turn them all off and the member only uses the app manually; turn them all on and they're the first to know when anything moves.
The Finance tab on a member's profile is where pay structure and earnings are configured and reviewed.

items.v: Hourly · Salary · Commission Only.
Required for hourly pay — used by Timesheet and Pay Runs.
Fixed amount for salaried members, also used by Pay Runs.
Weekly / bi-weekly / monthly — controls how Pay Runs batch.
Start and optional end date — leave end blank for an ongoing contract.
Percentage or flat amount per service/product line attributed to this member at checkout.
Overrides the default on specific services — useful for special rates on high-margin treatments.

blocks.text: The Earnings section is read-only — pick a date range and see total earnings, split between appointments and product sales.
Blocked Times is the Team-level overview of every non-bookable block across every member. Same data the Calendar shows — different angle, better for HR actions like cancelling a whole training day.

items: Create a block for any member — vacations, meetings, training, dentist appointments.
Repeat blocks daily, weekly, or monthly with a Repeat until date.
Delete with scope: this occurrence, this and future, or the whole series.

blocks.text: A read-only weekly grid of everyone's shifts, breaks, and approved leave in one view. Click a member name to jump to their individual schedule for edits. Useful for spotting coverage gaps before they hit the booking site.
Clock-in / clock-out tracking with break support. Can be used by each member on their phone, or by a manager at the front desk on everyone's behalf.

items.v: Starts the work timer for the day. Marked on-time or late against the scheduled shift.
Pauses the work timer.
Resumes the work timer.
Ends the day. Marked on-time or early.
If someone forgets to clock out, the system closes the shift at the end of their scheduled time and flags it.

blocks.text: The history view is filterable by member and date range. Missing records can be fixed with a Manual entry — useful for the first week when everyone forgets to clock in.
A weekly or custom-range comparison of scheduled vs actual hours, per member. Shows total hours, break minutes, overtime, late arrivals, and early departures — a single sheet that feeds payroll.

blocks.title: Period shortcuts
blocks.body: This Week and Last Week cover 95 percent of payroll cycles. Use Custom for one-off audits or when a pay run crosses a calendar boundary.

blocks.text: A pay run is a frozen batch of pay — one per pay period. It groups the hours, overtime, base pay, commission, bonuses, deductions, and net amount for each member into a single reviewable record.

items: Open Team → Pay Runs and click New Pay Run.
Pick the period and the members to include.
Review the draft. Edit bonuses or deductions, add notes where needed.
Submit for review. The data freezes.
Mark as Processed when you're ready to act on it. Mark as Paid when the money has gone out.
Back to Draft unlocks the pay run again if a mistake is caught.

blocks.title: Commission clawbacks
blocks.body: If a sale is refunded after the pay run is Paid, the commission becomes a negative line on the next pay run automatically. The deduction is flagged so you can discuss it with the affected team member.

blocks.text: Leave tracking lets team members request time off and managers approve or reject the requests. Balances are tracked per leave type.

items: A member opens Team → Leave and clicks Request Leave.
They pick the leave type (Annual, Sick, Unpaid, etc. — configured by the admin), the dates, and an optional reason.
A manager reviews the request. Approve adds it to the Team Schedule; Reject closes it with an optional note.
The member's balance updates; future requests check against the remaining quota.
Commission settings feed Sales, which feed Pay Runs. Set them up carefully once rather than editing pay runs row-by-row.
Copy schedule is your best friend — build one clean shift pattern and stamp it across similar roles.
Use Blocked Times, not Day Off, for known absences like a dentist appointment — the reason stays attached.
Run a Pay Run in Draft a week before payday. Fixing errors in draft is an order of magnitude easier than unpicking a Paid run.

accounting.title: Accounting
accounting.tagline: Your books. A double-entry ledger that auto-posts journal entries from every sale, refund, bill, and pay run — plus the tools to close the books: reconciliation, fixed assets, audit trail.
accounting.roles: The Accounting permission is usually restricted to the owner and a bookkeeper. Reading reports can be opened up; posting manual journal entries and approving refunds should stay locked down.

blocks.text: Momentfy keeps a proper double-entry ledger under the hood. Every time a sale is rung up, a refund processed, a bill paid, or a pay run posted, an equal-and-opposite journal entry is created automatically. The Accounting module is where you see and manage those books, plus do the things no ledger does on its own: reconciliations, fixed-asset depreciation, and audits.

The landing page shows the financial vital signs. Four KPIs at the top — Total Assets, Total Liabilities, Total Equity, Net Income MTD — sit next to two health checks that tell you whether the books still balance.

items.v: Debits equal credits across every account. If it's off, a manual journal entry or an import is the usual suspect.
The balance sheet equation. If it's off the accounts are structurally wrong; contact support before posting anything else.

blocks.text: Below the KPIs, the A/R and A/P aging summaries show outstanding receivables and payables bucketed by Current, 1–30, 31–60, 61–90, and 90+ days.
The Chart of Accounts is the backbone of the ledger. Every journal entry hits one or more of these accounts.

items: Momentfy ships a sensible default chart — you can start using it immediately.
Filter by Type (Asset, Liability, Equity, Revenue, Expense, Contra variants), Normal Balance (Debit or Credit), Origin (System or Custom), and Active/Inactive status.
Create a custom account when you need a level of detail Momentfy doesn't ship by default — for example a subsidiary revenue account for a new service line.
Deactivating an account hides it from the selector without deleting historical entries.

blocks.text: Every transaction lives here. The Source column tells you what created each entry: SALE, INVOICE_ISSUE, INVOICE_PAYMENT, EXPENSE, SUPPLIER_PAYMENT, BILL, GIFT_CARD_ISSUE, CASH_REGISTER_CLOSE, DEPRECIATION, PAYROLL, or MANUAL.

items: Expand any entry for the line-by-line breakdown of debits and credits. Both sides must balance.
Filter by Source and date range.
Click New Journal Entry to post a manual entry — pick accounts, enter debit and credit amounts, add a description. The Save button is disabled until both sides balance.

blocks.title: Manual entries are for exceptions
blocks.body: Any auto-posted entry has a traceable source. A manual entry doesn't. Only post manual entries for things the app genuinely can't handle — opening balances, bank fees, accruals — and always include a descriptive memo.

blocks.text: When you migrate from another system, Opening Balances is how you seed the ledger with starting amounts.

items: Open Accounting → Opening Balances.
Enter the balance for each account, grouped by Asset, Liability, Equity.
Any residual difference between debits and credits auto-balances into Owner's Equity — the chart's clean-up account.
Submit. The balances post as a single journal entry sourced from OPENING_BALANCES.

blocks.title: Do this once
blocks.body: Re-running opening balances creates a second posting on top of your history. If you need to correct a value, create an adjusting journal entry instead.

blocks.text: Bills are what you owe — supplier invoices, utility bills, any money that's due out but not paid yet.

items: Open Accounting → Bills and click New Bill.
Pick the supplier, enter the bill number (the supplier's own reference), issue date, and due date.
Add line items — description, amount, and the expense account it hits.
Save as Draft while you check it; move to Open to register the liability.
Record payments from Sales → Supplier Payments or from the bill detail; the status moves to Partially Paid or Paid automatically.

items.v: Editable, not on the books yet.
Owing; counts toward A/P Aging.
Some payment received; remainder still in A/P.
Closed; zero balance.
Cancelled — reverses any accounting impact.

blocks.text: Big-ticket purchases that you'll use for years — equipment, furniture, vehicles, computers. The Fixed Assets module tracks each one and posts monthly depreciation automatically so your net income isn't hit all at once.

items: Open Accounting → Fixed Assets and click New Asset.
Enter name, category, acquisition date, cost, salvage value, and useful life in months.
Pick a depreciation method: Straight-line (equal monthly charge) or Declining balance (front-loaded with a declining rate).
A preview shows the monthly and annual charge plus the book value at end of life.
Save. A journal entry posts for the asset; monthly depreciation entries auto-post going forward.

blocks.text: Disposing of an asset
When you sell or retire an asset, click Dispose on its row. Enter the disposal date and the proceeds; the system posts the gain or loss against the book value and closes the asset.
Reconciliation is how you prove the ledger matches the bank. Import the bank statement, match each line to a recorded payment, and close the period.

items: Open Accounting → Bank Reconciliation and pick a bank account.
Upload the statement CSV. Map the columns: Date, Description, Reference, and either a signed Amount or separate Debit and Credit columns.
Momentfy auto-matches rows to recorded payments and expenses within ±7 days by amount.
Match unmatched lines by hand — click the row, pick the corresponding ledger entry.
Watch the reconciliation badge: it shows In balance or Out of balance by X so you know where you stand.
When it's in balance, save the reconciliation for the audit trail.

blocks.text: Every journal entry is hashed in a chain — change anything, even a description, and the chain breaks. The Audit Trail page lets an auditor or an owner re-verify the whole ledger.

items: The page shows the row count verified and any hash mismatches.
Click Re-verify to recompute the chain — takes seconds even on large ledgers.
Mismatches are rare and usually mean a bug in an import, not tampering — but they flag it for investigation.

blocks.text: Refunds above your approval threshold land here instead of completing immediately. The rule: the team member who raised the refund cannot be the one who approves it.

items: Open Accounting → Pending Refunds.
Review each pending refund: the original sale, the reason, the raiser.
Approve to release the refund to the client — the sale's refund status completes and the journal entry posts.
Reject with a reason if the refund shouldn't go through. The original sale stays completed as-is.

blocks.title: Thresholds
blocks.body: Set the approval amount under Settings → Approvals. Set it low enough to catch the refunds that matter, high enough not to drown your day in approvals.

items: Reconcile monthly. A year of unreconciled statements is a world of pain; 30 days is a coffee.
Use categories on every expense and bill — category filters make the P&L actually readable.
Keep manual journal entries rare and annotated. Future-you will thank present-you for the memo.
Re-run the audit trail after any bulk import or data migration — it's the cheapest guarantee the books are intact.

reports.title: Reports
reports.tagline: Every question your business asks, answered. Financial, operational, team, inventory, and AI-summarised reports, all filterable by date and branch, exportable to PDF.
reports.roles: Each report has its own permission (Sales reports, Finance reports, Team reports, etc.) so you can open Performance to managers but keep Profit & Loss to the owner.

blocks.text: Reports is a big menu of pre-built dashboards and an even bigger one of financial statements. Each report is a single-page view with a handful of charts and tables, plus filters for date, branch, and comparison. Every report can be exported to PDF and optionally summarised by the built-in AI assistant.
Reports → All Reports is the navigation page. Reports are grouped into categories:

items: Operational: Performance, Staff, Clients, Attendance, Resources.
Financial: Profit & Loss, Trial Balance, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, A/R & A/P Aging.
Tax: VAT Report, Discounts & Promos.
Inventory: Stock Report, Commission Payout.
Medical (healthcare only): Medical Records, Prescriptions, Lab Orders.

blocks.text: Each card has a short description. Click View report to open.
Every report shares the same filter bar at the top.

items.v: Presets: Today, Yesterday, Last 7 days, Last 30 days, This Month, Last Month — plus a Custom picker for any span.
Compare to the previous period (of equal length), to the same period last year, or show no comparison at all.
All locations or a single branch if you have multiple.

blocks.title: Comparison changes everything
blocks.body: A 2% drop in sales week-on-week is different from a 2% drop versus the same week last year. Switch comparison modes to pressure-test the story.

blocks.text: The flagship report. KPIs for Total Sales, Services, Products, Memberships, No-shows, Cancellations, Average Sale Value, Appointment Count, Occupancy Rate, and Returning Client Rate. Time-series charts for each. Sales-by-channel breakdown so you see how much came through cash, card, bank transfer, online, and installments.

A per-team-member table of revenue, commission earned, and appointment count — with totals at the top. The starting point for any conversation about who's over-booked, who has headroom, and how commissions are shaping up.
New clients, returning clients, top spenders, most frequent visitors, inactive clients (no visit in 60 days), and active memberships. A quick read on retention health.

Revenue (ex. tax) minus Cost of Goods Sold gives Gross Profit. Gross Profit minus staff commissions and operating expenses gives Operating Profit. Operating Profit minus taxes and interest gives Net Profit. Everything expandable to see account-level detail.

Output VAT collected on sales by category (Standard, Zero, Exempt), input VAT reclaimable on bills, and the net VAT payable. ZATCA-ready formatting for Saudi Arabia; ETA-ready for Egypt. Export as PDF and hand it to your accountant or submit directly.
Finance Overview gives you a snapshot of the money: gross revenue, net revenue after tax and discounts, tax collected, refunds, tips, outstanding A/R and A/P, and a payment-method breakdown.

Daily Cash Register is the drill-down: per day and per location, the cash movements — cash in, card in, cash out, other in — and the net movement. Filters for location make multi-branch reconciliation fast.

A/R Aging buckets every outstanding customer balance by age: Current, 1–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+. A/P Aging does the same for supplier balances. Both lead with totals, then drop into per-customer/supplier detail.

Active products count, low-stock item count (with an alert badge when it's non-zero), top products by revenue, and total stock value at cost. Each product row shows current stock, reorder level, and a status indicator.
Most reports have an AI Summary button at the top. Click it and the built-in assistant reads the numbers, highlights the changes worth knowing, and writes a short paragraph explaining the story.

items: Summaries expire after a short window — re-generate if the underlying data has changed.
Export to PDF saves both the report and the AI summary (if present).
Export respects the current filter — what you see is exactly what lands in the PDF.
Bookmark the date-range-plus-comparison combinations you actually use. A URL with the params set is re-openable on any device.
Export the VAT report monthly and file it in a cloud folder. Tax authorities love a consistent trail.
AI summaries are a starting point, not a conclusion. Read the numbers alongside the text.
If a report looks wrong, hit refresh — each report shows its data-freshness timestamp at the top.

portal.title: Client portal
portal.tagline: Your shop's public, branded booking site and client self-service area. Your clients see your colours, your services, your team — not Momentfy.
portal.roles: The portal is public — no login required to browse and book. Clients can optionally create an account to see their history, manage bookings, and access loyalty.

blocks.text: The Client portal is a branded mini-site per tenant. Your cover photo, your colours, your gallery, your team. Clients can browse services, book, pay, and — if they sign in — see their full history, manage bookings, redeem loyalty, and pick up medical documents. Every URL is under /book/<your-shop-slug> or /portal/<your-shop-slug>; you can also point a custom domain at it.
The front page leads with your cover image, business name, star rating, and primary location. Below, tabs for everything on offer.

items.v: Browsable catalogue with duration and price; Smart Pricing rules adjust live.
Retail items with variants, add-to-cart, and an online checkout.
Recurring plans — clients can subscribe directly.
Photo, name, title, and rating for each member available for booking.
Latest client reviews, business replies, and a see-all link.
Business description, opening hours, contact, and location map.

blocks.text: The booking flow is four steps, none of them required to be logged in.

items: Pick a service. Search by name or browse by category.
Pick a professional. Individual members appear with ratings, or No preference for maximum availability.
Pick a date and time. Dates with no availability are greyed out; times within a date show only real open slots.
Confirm. Client details (name, email, phone) if not signed in, booking summary, payment method (pay at venue, card, Tabby/Tamara installments), and an optional promo code.

blocks.title: Promo codes
blocks.body: The promo code field lives on the confirmation step. Valid codes apply the discount immediately and show the new total. Invalid codes show a friendly error — the booking doesn't submit until it's resolved.

blocks.text: Once confirmed, the client lands on a success page showing everything they need.

items: Booking reference — the last 8 characters of the appointment ID.
Service, professional, date, time, and location.
QR code for the front desk to scan on arrival.
Action buttons: Add to Calendar (.ics download), Download PDF, Print, View confirmation.
A Cancel Booking button if your cancellation policy allows it.

blocks.text: The confirmation email contains a unique reschedule/cancel link. Clicking it (or using My Bookings if the client is signed in) opens a simplified version of the booking wizard.

items: Reschedule: pick a new date and time from the same slot calendar. Confirm to move the booking.
Cancel: a two-step confirmation to avoid misclicks. On confirm, the appointment is cancelled and the slot is released.

blocks.title: Cancellation policy
blocks.body: If your policy blocks last-minute cancellations (set under Settings → Online Booking), the button is disabled inside the cutoff window. The client sees a clear reason and your contact details.

blocks.text: Clients don't need an account to book — but signing in unlocks history, memberships, loyalty, and faster checkout.

items.v: First name, last name, email, optional phone, and a password. Creates a new profile or links to an existing one if the email is recognised.
Email + password. A Forgot password link sends a reset email.
If the client has booked before without a password, the first login prompts them to pick one — existing history then attaches to the account.

blocks.text: Signed-in clients see every booking they've ever made, filtered by tab: All, Upcoming, Past, Cancelled. Each card shows the service, date/time, professional, location, status, and notes. Reschedule and Cancel buttons appear whenever allowed.

After a completed appointment, the client can leave a review — a 5-star rating and an optional comment. Pending tab lists appointments waiting for a review; Completed tab shows the reviews already submitted. Businesses can respond to reviews from their dashboard; responses show up on the same card.

Self-service for core details — first name, last name, phone — and a password change. Anything more sensitive (identity documents, medical information) lives in the main client profile that only staff can edit.

If you've enabled the Loyalty add-on, clients see their current points balance and a running transaction log (earned, redeemed, expired, adjusted). Memberships are listed with their current period, appointments remaining, renewal date, and the plan's benefits.
Invoices always appear for any client with formal invoices issued — number, date, amount, status, and a downloadable PDF.

For healthcare tenants, the portal also exposes Prescriptions and Lab Results. Each is read-only: the prescribing doctor, drugs, dispense status for prescriptions; the ordered tests, results, clinician's notes for labs.
Some pages are public and don't require login — useful for sharing a receipt, an invoice, or an estimate over WhatsApp.

items.v: A shareable booking confirmation / receipt view. Includes QR code and payment status.
A token-protected invoice PDF with line items and payment instructions.
Quote review before the client commits — includes Accept/Decline actions.

items: Hero photo, gallery, and description are the first thing a new client sees — a hour spent on them converts much better than another promo code.
Keep service descriptions specific. "45-min deep cleanse facial with senior esthetician" wins over "Facial".
Turn on auto-confirm only once you trust your catalogue. Manual review catches double bookings and odd requests in the early days.
Point a custom domain at the portal as soon as you can — clients treat a branded URL very differently from a third-party slug.

settings.title: Settings
settings.tagline: Everything that shapes how your Momentfy workspace behaves — branding, locations, payments, receipts, notifications, integrations, and backups.
settings.roles: Full Settings access is typically owner-only. Individual areas (Locations, Receipts, Notifications) can be delegated via custom permissions.

blocks.text: Settings is the control panel. Most of it you'll set once during onboarding and rarely touch again; a few areas (tax rates, locations, notifications) you'll come back to as the business grows. Every change here applies tenant-wide — every team member sees the new behaviour as soon as they reload.

The root identity of your workspace — name, logo, country, and currency. Also the team and client default languages (controls the UI language for new team members and the default booking site language) and number-format preferences (Western 1,234.56 vs Arabic-Indic ١٢٣٤٫٥٦).

items.v: All appointments, sales, and reports are stored in UTC but rendered in this timezone.
Pick the one your staff and clients are used to reading.
Monday or Sunday. Feeds the Calendar's week view and the Month view grid.

blocks.text: See the Calendar module docs for the full behaviour. The key switches live here:

items: Display processing time blocks — shows the pre/post buffer on the grid.
Display blocked time — shows blocked-time blocks on the grid (still blocks online bookings either way).
Allow booking outside schedule — lets staff create appointments outside the member's shift.
Allow booking in past — lets staff backfill appointments that already happened.
Allow overbooking — lets staff double-book the same slot.
Auto no-show — automatically marks late appointments as No-Show after a grace period.
Appointment status colours — customise the colour for each status.

blocks.text: Toggle which payment methods appear at checkout: Cash, Card, Bank Transfer, Online, Installments (Tabby, Tamara), Gift Card, Loyalty Points, Insurance, Other. Disabled methods don't show up on the POS or the booking portal.

items: Open Settings → Tax Rates and click Add Tax Rate.
Give it a name (VAT 15%), the rate percentage, and mark it as Default if it should auto-apply to new services and products.
Save. The rate shows up in the catalogue tax dropdown.

blocks.title: Inclusive or exclusive
blocks.body: The toggle under Business Info → "Retail prices include tax" decides whether your catalogue prices already include VAT or not. Flip it once at the start — changing it later means repricing the whole catalogue.

blocks.text: Pick the receipt template (A4 Standard or POS Thermal), show or hide the logo, and add header/footer text. Auto-send receipt via email toggles whether completed sales trigger an automatic email to the client. Preview renders a sample receipt so you can eyeball it before committing.

Enable tipping at checkout and provide preset percentages (e.g. 10, 15, 20). Clients see those as quick buttons plus a Custom option. Tips attribute to the team member on the sale — feed through to commissions and pay runs.
Configure the public booking portal — the thing your clients see.

items: Enable / disable the whole portal.
Max advance booking (days ahead) and minimum notice (hours).
Booking slot interval — 15, 30, or 60 minutes.
Booking page description, cover image, and gallery photos.
Cancellation policy text.
Auto-confirm toggle to skip the manual review step.
Show products / memberships / reviews on the booking site.
Custom domain CNAME — point your own domain at the portal.
Live booking link — copyable URL for sharing.

blocks.text: Multi-branch? This is where each branch is defined.

items: Open Settings → Locations and click Add Location.
Fill in the basics: name, description, photo.
Contact info: phone, email.
Address: street, city, state, postal code, country. Pin the map location for directions.
Timezone, capacity (max simultaneous appointments), and business hours per day.
Assign the team members who work there.
Save. Mark one location as the Default for cases where a location isn't explicitly chosen.

blocks.text: Connect a Zoom account and online appointments auto-generate Zoom links — no more copy/paste into the notes field. Toggles for waiting room, cloud recording, and which appointment types auto-generate links. Connect is an OAuth flow; the connected account email is shown once connected.

Where uploaded files (photos, logos, receipts) are stored. Local disk is the default — easy, no setup, but the server grows with your data. S3-compatible (AWS S3, DigitalOcean Spaces, MinIO) or Cloudinary are the two alternatives; both offload storage and serve images via CDN.

blocks.title: Migration
blocks.body: Changing storage providers after you've already uploaded content is non-trivial. Pick your target at the start if you can.

blocks.text: Customize the dashboard sidebar. Reorder items, hide what you don't use, promote frequently-used add-ons to the top level, and pin Quick Access links to specific pages. Reset to defaults undoes everything.

Build permission presets once, apply them to every new member. Useful if you have the same role across many staff ("Senior Stylist", "Reception") with the same access pattern. Templates are editable later; updating a template does not auto-apply to members already set up from it.
Compliance-friendly purge rules.

items.v: How long soft-deleted rows are kept before being purged.
How long read notifications are retained.
How long activity logs are kept (0 = forever).
Retention for these specific records.
Lets clients request full deletion of their data from the portal.

blocks.text: Buy Now Pay Later — Tabby and Tamara. Enable one or both, choose sandbox or live, enter the credentials provided by the BNPL partner (public key, secret key, webhook secret, API token), and pick the number of installments. Test connection sanity-checks the integration before you take a live payment.
Full business export and import.

items: Export: download a JSON backup that includes clients, services, products, appointments, sales, inventory, locations, discounts, memberships, schedule, ZATCA data, gift cards, reviews, activity logs, and settings.
Restore: upload a previously exported backup to replace the current workspace data with the backup contents. The system asks for a confirmation matching the source business name to prevent accidents.
Team members are matched by user account on restore — they're not included as data rows.

blocks.title: Restore is destructive
blocks.body: A restore replaces the current workspace data. Always back up the current state first, then restore. For partial imports, use the CSV imports in Clients or Catalogue instead.

blocks.text: Your personal account credentials — separate from workspace settings. Current password, new password, confirm, save. Use a password manager; never share the owner account.

items: Set up Business Info, Date & Time, Tax Rates, and Payment Methods first. Every other area depends on those.
Keep receipts crisp — logo, phone, and a one-line thank-you are enough. Busy receipts get thrown away.
Data retention should align with your local laws and your industry norms. Keep longer than required if unsure.
Run an export before any major configuration change. If you break something, the worst case is a minute-old restore.

zatca.title: ZATCA — Saudi e-invoicing
zatca.tagline: Stay compliant with the Saudi Zakat, Tax and Customs Authority. Certificate onboarding, UBL signing, QR stamping, and real-time reporting — all inside Momentfy.
zatca.roles: Needs the Add-ons permission to enable and configure. Once live, every sale is auto-reported — the team just rings up as usual.

blocks.text: Every VAT-registered business in Saudi Arabia has to comply with ZATCA's e-invoicing rules — Phase 1 for printable/PDF invoices with a QR stamp, Phase 2 for signed UBL documents submitted live to the tax authority. This add-on handles all of it: generating the Certificate Signing Request, uploading CSIDs, signing invoices, stamping QR codes, and submitting each invoice with status tracking.
It runs entirely on your own server — private keys never leave your infrastructure.

items: Open Dashboard → Add-ons and find ZATCA.
Click Activate. The add-on appears under Add-ons → ZATCA in your sidebar.
Open ZATCA. If you have multiple locations, pick the one you want to configure — each location has its own certificate.
The page has three tabs: Settings, Onboarding, Reporting. Work through them in order.

blocks.text: The Settings tab captures everything ZATCA needs to know about the legal entity behind this location.

items: Business name in Arabic and English.
VAT number (15 digits) and Commercial Registration Number (CRN).
Full street address: street, city, district, postal code, building number, additional number.
Environment toggle: Sandbox for testing, Production for live invoices.

blocks.title: Prefill from the location
blocks.body: If you've already filled the location address under Settings → Locations, use Prefill from Location to pull it in. If this is your second branch using the same entity, Copy from Location clones another location's ZATCA config.

blocks.text: ZATCA uses a two-stage certificate model — first a Compliance CSID for testing, then a Production CSID for live submissions. The add-on walks you through the whole handshake.

items: Generate CSR — Momentfy creates a Certificate Signing Request tied to your VAT number and CRN.
Compliance CSID — paste the OTP from the ZATCA Fatoora portal. Momentfy exchanges it for a compliance certificate.
Compliance Check — Momentfy sends a few sample invoices to ZATCA's sandbox. A green Completed badge means the pipeline works end-to-end.
Production CSID — after compliance passes, upgrade to a production certificate. From this moment your invoices are live.

blocks.title: Certificate expiry
blocks.body: Certificates expire. The Onboarding tab shows the expiration date; rotate before it hits zero or submissions start failing.

blocks.text: Once certificates are in place, every sale auto-generates a ZATCA invoice document. The Reporting tab is the control panel.

items: Stat cards: Pending, Accepted, Rejected, Warning, Total.
Report Now pushes all currently-pending invoices to ZATCA in one batch.
