View Invoices opens the full invoice list — search, filter by status, open any invoice for its UUID, ICV, submission status, and error/warning details.
Inline actions per row: Regenerate & Report, Retry Report — useful when a single invoice fails.

blocks.text: A small share of invoices will get Rejected or land in Warning state — the error is attached to the invoice detail. Common causes: a client VAT number typo, an address missing a required field, or a transient network error on ZATCA's side.

items: Transient network errors retry automatically via an hourly cron.
Hard rejections (data issues) stay pending until you fix the underlying sale and click Regenerate & Report.
Every attempt is kept on the invoice record, so there's always an audit trail.
Always start in Sandbox. A failed compliance check is free there; a failed production invoice is a corrected sale.
Rotate certificates well before expiry — set a calendar reminder one month ahead.
Run Report Now at end of day — the hourly cron handles it passively, but a manual push catches anything stuck.

eta.title: ETA — Egypt e-invoicing
eta.tagline: Submit B2B invoices to Egypt's Tax Authority. Upload your certificate, map your codes, and have every sale clear the ETA portal automatically.
eta.roles: Add-ons permission to set up; no permission needed to ring up sales once it's live.

blocks.text: Egypt's ETA requires every B2B invoice to be mapped to item and customer codes, signed, and submitted to the authority portal. The add-on builds the ETA document structure, signs it with your uploaded certificate, and tracks its status from Draft → Submitted → Valid (or Invalid, with errors attached for you to fix).

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → ETA → Activate.
Open the ETA page. If you have multiple locations, pick the one you're configuring — ETA is per-location because tax branches differ.
Three tabs: Settings, Onboarding, Invoices. Settings first.
Tax Registration Number, Activity Code, Branch ID.
Company name in Arabic and English.
Full Egyptian address: governorate, region/city, street, building number, postal code, floor, room.
Environment: Preproduction for testing, Production for live.
API Credentials: Client ID and Client Secret from your ETA developer account.

blocks.title: Prefill & copy
blocks.body: Like ZATCA, you can Prefill address from the location and Copy from another location if more than one branch shares a legal entity.

items: Step 1: Configure Settings — this is ticked once you've saved a valid settings tab.
Step 2: Upload Certificate — upload a PKCS#12 (.p12 or .pfx) certificate and enter the password. Once uploaded, the card shows the serial, issuer, and expiration date.
Step 3: Test Connection — Momentfy hits the ETA API with your credentials and certificate. Green Completed means live.

blocks.text: The Invoices tab lists every ETA invoice with its current state. Filters: Draft, Pending, Submitted, Valid, Invalid, Cancelled.

items: Submit Now pushes all Draft/Pending invoices in one batch.
Per-row Send button submits a single invoice.
For Submitted or Valid invoices, the ✕ button opens a Cancel dialog — type a reason and confirm. The ETA portal accepts cancellations with reason codes.
The invoice detail modal shows the internal ID, ETA UUID, Long ID, full error list, and per-attempt submission timeline.

blocks.title: Invalid needs fixing
blocks.body: Invalid status means ETA rejected the document — usually a missing item or customer code. Fix the sale, then click Retry Submit on the invoice.

items: Map all your top-selling SKUs to ETA item codes before going live. An unmapped item at the counter blocks the submission.
Run a few sandbox submissions end-to-end before switching to Production — most rejections come from schema surprises, not from your setup.
The hourly retry handles transient failures; don't keep re-pressing Submit.

google-calendar.title: Google Calendar
google-calendar.tagline: Two-way sync between Momentfy appointments and Google Calendar. Every team member sees their day in whichever calendar they already live in.
google-calendar.roles: Add-ons permission to set up; each team member connects (or disconnects) their own Google account themselves.

blocks.text: The Google Calendar add-on keeps two calendars in sync. Create an appointment in Momentfy and it shows up on the team member's Google Calendar. Reschedule in Google and Momentfy updates. Cancel either side and the other follows.
It works per team member and per location — everyone uses their own Google account, and each location can also sync to a shared business calendar.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Google Calendar → Activate.
Open Add-ons → Google Calendar. Everything configures on a single settings page.

blocks.text: Managed (SaaS) users skip this section — the credentials are already set up for them. Self-hosted operators need to create a Google Cloud project and supply their own OAuth client.

items: Create a project at console.cloud.google.com.
Enable the Google Calendar API.
Create OAuth credentials (Web application type).
Copy the Client ID and Client Secret into the Google API Credentials card.
Copy the Redirect URI shown on the card into your Google Cloud console as an authorised redirect URI.
Save.

items.v: Push new appointments to Google Calendar as events.
Update the Google event when the appointment moves.
Remove the Google event when the appointment is cancelled.
Attach the client as a Google Calendar attendee — they receive an email invite and can add it to their own calendar.
Mark the event as busy on Google, so scheduling apps see the team member as unavailable.
Minutes before the event to trigger a Google Calendar notification. 30 minutes is the default.

items: In Connected Staff Accounts, click the blue Connect link next to a team member.
They're redirected to Google, sign in, and grant calendar permission.
When they return, the row shows their Google email and a green Connected badge.
Repeat for Business Calendars — each location can sync to a shared Google calendar (useful for a front-desk screen).
Sync All at the top forces an immediate pull from Google for every connected account — helpful after bulk-importing events.

blocks.title: Token Expired
blocks.body: If a staff member changes their Google password or revokes access, their row shows Token Expired. They just click Link to re-grant — no data lost.

items: Turn on Send Client Invites for online appointments — combined with Zoom, the client gets a calendar event with the meeting link already inside.
Keep Block Busy Times on. Otherwise, Google-driven schedulers (Meet, Calendly) will book over your Momentfy appointments.
Sync All is a one-off tool for onboarding. Don't run it on a cron — the two-way sync handles ongoing updates fine.

ai-assistant.title: AI Assistant
ai-assistant.tagline: A configurable AI layer across the app. Chat on the booking site and portal, automated report summaries, and vision analysis for medical images.
ai-assistant.roles: Add-ons permission to set up. Every team member sees the features (report summaries, clinical AI hints) once the add-on is on.

blocks.text: The AI Assistant is a single configuration that powers every AI feature in the app: chat on the client booking site, chat in the client portal, the report summaries you see above every dashboard, and vision-based hints for uploaded clinical images.
You pick the provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, LMStudio, or a custom endpoint), the model, the language, and the tone. The same settings apply everywhere the AI is used.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → AI Assistant → Activate.
Open Add-ons → AI Assistant. Toggle Enabled at the top of the settings page.
Configure Provider, Model, and the rest as described below, then Save.

items.v: Base URL fixed to OpenAI's endpoint. Paste your API key. Models load automatically from your account.
Same as OpenAI but for Claude. API key required.
Local. Point the Base URL at your local Ollama server (default http://localhost:11434/v1). No API key. Click Refresh to reload the list of installed models.
Local. Similar to Ollama — point at the LMStudio server URL.
Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Useful for proxies or custom deployments.

blocks.title: Vision support
blocks.body: For Clinical Imaging AI hints and photo analysis, pick a model that lists vision support. The model dropdown shows context window and modality so you can tell at a glance.

items: Dialect — the language the AI replies in. English, Modern Standard Arabic, Egyptian Arabic, Saudi Arabic, and more.
Tone — friendly, professional, casual. Tweaks the personality without retraining.
Max Messages Per Session (1–100) caps the conversation length per chat window. 30 is a sensible default.
System Prompt Suffix is a free-text field appended to the AI's system prompt. Use it to add house rules — "Never quote prices; always redirect pricing to the front desk," for example.
Client booking site — a chat widget for FAQs and rescheduling.
Client portal — logged-in clients chat for self-service.
Reports — every report has a Generate AI Summary button at the top.
Clinical imaging — the vision model analyses uploaded X-rays and photos (Clinical Imaging add-on must also be on).
Medical records — AI-suggested diagnoses on draft records (Medical Records add-on must also be on).
Self-host Ollama if you can't send data to a cloud provider — the local provider option keeps everything on your server.
Use the System Prompt Suffix to encode your brand voice and any compliance rules.
Start with a small model and upgrade if you feel the responses are shallow. Bigger models are slower and more expensive without always being better for short business queries.

insurance.title: Insurance
insurance.tagline: Insurance providers, coverage plans, and claim tracking. Auto-generate claims on checkout, batch-submit to payers, watch the status until paid.
insurance.roles: Add-ons permission to set up. Front-desk staff only need the Insurance permission to open claims and link coverage at the POS.

blocks.text: Insurance turns Momentfy from a cash-only POS into a full payer workflow. Set up the providers you accept (AXA, Bupa, Allianz…), define their plans and coverage percentages, and every sale with an insured client automatically becomes a claim with the covered portion pre-calculated and the client copay displayed at checkout.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Insurance → Activate.
Set up providers and plans before going live — see next sections.
Link each client to their insurance from the client profile's Insurance tab.
Add-ons → Insurance → Providers.
Add Provider. Name is enough to get started — "AXA Insurance", "Bupa", etc.
Edit any provider with the pencil icon; delete with the trash icon (only if no claims exist on it).

blocks.text: Every provider has one or more coverage plans (sometimes called classes) — Gold 100%, Silver 80%, etc. Each plan defines the share of a sale the insurer covers.

items: Open a provider → Classes.
Add a plan with a name, coverage percentage, and any copay or cap rules.
Save.

items.v: When on, every sale with an insured client auto-generates a claim in Draft state.
Prefix for claim numbers — "INS" gives INS-001, INS-002, and so on.
Fallback percentage used if a client is insured but no plan is selected — usually 80.

blocks.text: The Claims page is where the day-to-day happens. Filter by status, date, or search by client.

items: Select one or more drafts with the checkboxes and hit Batch Submit — the add-on packages them up for the payer.
Click any row to open the claim detail slide-over: covered amount, client copay, attachments, status history.
Status flow: Draft → Submitted → Approved / Rejected → Paid.
Rejections can be edited and resubmitted; Approved claims wait on payment.
Add every plan even if you think they're the same — payers reconcile on plan names, and a mismatch is a manual support call.
Keep the Claim Number Prefix stable. Payers reference it; changing it mid-stream is pain.
Batch-submit at the end of the day, not after every sale. One batch is much easier to reconcile than 40 single submissions.

events.title: Events & Ticketing
events.tagline: Paid events, classes, and workshops. Create events with ticket types, publish to the booking site, check in attendees by QR.
events.roles: Add-ons permission to manage. Front-desk staff only need permissions to check in attendees.

blocks.text: Events are anything clients pay for that isn't a standard appointment — a yoga class, a workshop, a seminar, a paid consultation evening. Each event has a start and end time, a capacity, one or more ticket types, and its own cover image and description on the booking site.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Events & Ticketing → Activate.
Open Add-ons → Events. The main page lists every event.
Optionally visit Settings first to configure waitlist, guest checkout, QR prefix, and check-in notifications.
Click New Event.
Fill in the basics: title, description, cover image, event type (in-person / online / hybrid), location, start and end date-time, timezone, total capacity, organiser.

Enable the right advanced options — Zoom integration for online events, Require Approval for invite-only sessions, Allow Guest Checkout for frictionless purchase, Enable Waitlist to keep demand visible once the event sells out.
Save as Draft while you iterate.

blocks.text: Each event has one or more ticket types — an Early Bird at a discount, a Standard price, a VIP with a higher cap. Set each ticket's name, price, and capacity. The total capacity at the event level still wins if ticket caps would push you over it.

blocks.title: Pricing mix
blocks.body: Early Bird + Standard pairs are the most common — set the Early Bird with a low cap (say 20) and the Standard with the rest. When Early Bird sells out, the booking site shows only Standard.

blocks.text: Drafts are private — you can review, share internal preview links, and iterate. Publish flips the event live on the client booking site.

items: Publish — the event appears in the client booking site's Events tab.
Unpublish — takes it back to draft without deleting.
Cancel — refunds (configurable) go back to every ticket holder; the event is archived.

blocks.text: When the event runs, open Events → the event → Check-In. Two flows:

items: QR scan — point your camera at the client's ticket QR code. Instantly marked as arrived.
Manual lookup — search by name or ticket ID; tick the checkbox on the attendee list.

blocks.text: The page updates live — attended count, waitlist size, capacity bar. No-shows can be marked at the end so they land in your reports.

items: Always publish with the cover image set. An empty grey card converts at a fraction of a styled event.
Use the waitlist even if the event isn't sold out — it signals interest and you can shift to a bigger venue if demand warrants.
Export the attendee list as CSV the night before — useful as a paper backup if the check-in screen has network trouble.

resources.title: Resources
resources.tagline: Bookable rooms, chairs, and equipment. Tie resources to services so the Calendar never double-books the treatment room.
resources.roles: Add-ons permission to manage. Staff see resources auto-assigned on the Calendar with no extra permissions.

blocks.text: A resource is anything bookable that isn't a person — a treatment room, a dental chair, a machine, a shared laptop. Resources extend the Calendar with a second axis: an appointment needs the right team member and the right resource to go ahead.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Resources → Activate.
Open Add-ons → Resources. The landing page is a live dashboard of today's reservations grouped by location.

blocks.text: Start by creating the types of resource you own. A type has a name, an icon, and a colour — so the Calendar can visually distinguish a Room from a Chair from a Machine.

items: Open the Types tab.
Add Type — name, icon, colour.
Save. Repeat for each category.
Back on the main page, click Add Resource.
Pick the type, give the resource a name ("Room A", "Chair 2"), and assign it to a location.
Save. The resource now shows up in the Calendar and on the Timeline.

blocks.text: A service that needs a room (a massage, a dental procedure) should have that resource requirement recorded. Open the service in the Catalogue → scroll to Resources → add one or more types or specific resources.

blocks.title: Requiring a type vs. a specific resource
blocks.body: Require a Type ("Any treatment room") for flexibility. Require a specific resource ("Room A") only when the service genuinely can't run elsewhere — an imaging machine, say.

blocks.text: The Timeline tab is a horizontal calendar where each row is a resource and each block is a reservation or a manual block. Useful for seeing the room plan at a glance and for blocking time when a resource is out of service.

items: Drag on an empty row to create a block (maintenance, deep clean, repair).
Click any block to edit or delete it.
Date picker in the header switches the day.

items.v: When on, a booking that requires a type auto-picks the first free resource of that type. Turn off if you want staff to pick deliberately.
Blocks saving an appointment that has no resource assigned.
Paints resource assignments on the main Calendar grid.
A cool-down window between bookings on the same resource — time to clean, reset, turn over.

items: Start with types, not named resources. A type with three rooms under it covers the real world; three disconnected rooms don't tell the scheduler anything.
Set a 10-15 minute buffer for anything that needs cleaning. Back-to-back bookings look efficient in theory and cause chaos in practice.
Block the room on the Timeline for deep cleans and maintenance. The Calendar then won't allow bookings even if a member is free.

loyalty-points.title: Loyalty Points
loyalty-points.tagline: A points-based loyalty program with configurable earn, redeem, expiry, and bonus rules. Clients earn on checkout, redeem for discount.
loyalty-points.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Front-desk staff redeem points at checkout with the Sales permission.

blocks.text: Loyalty Points keeps clients coming back. Earn rate converts spend into points at checkout. Redeem rate converts points back into discount at the next visit. Bonus rules let you double or triple earnings on specific categories, memberships, or birthdays.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Loyalty Points → Activate.
Open Add-ons → Loyalty Points. The dashboard shows Issued, Redeemed, Expired, and Active Clients.
Go to Settings to configure before promoting the program.

items.v: Points per unit of currency spent. 1 means "1 point per 1 SAR". Half that (0.5) means "1 point per 2 SAR".
Minimum sale total before points are awarded. 0 means any sale earns. Set to, say, 50 to only award on sales above that amount.

blocks.text: Points are awarded when the sale is marked Completed, not when it's created. Refunds take points back.

items.v: How much discount each point is worth. 100 points = 10 SAR means 1 point = 0.1 SAR.
Minimum balance the client must have before they can redeem. 200 is a sensible floor — below that, redemptions feel like noise.

blocks.text: At checkout, add Loyalty Points as a payment method. The POS shows the client's balance and applies the discount.

items.v: Days from earning before points expire. 365 (one year) is standard. 0 disables expiry.
Days before expiry when the client is notified. 30 gives time for one more visit.

blocks.text: Bonus rules multiply the earn rate in specific conditions. Examples:

items: Category bonus — 2x points on Haircut services.
Membership bonus — 2x for all members, 3x for Gold members.
Birthday bonus — 3x points during the client's birthday month.
Settings → Bonus Rules → Add Rule.
Pick the trigger (Category / Membership / Birthday).
Set the multiplier (2x, 3x).
Save.

blocks.text: The Transactions tab is a full ledger of everything: Earn, Redeem, Expire, Adjust, Refund. Filter by type, date, or client. Click any row for the details.
Each client's profile shows their current balance and their own transaction history on a Loyalty Points tab — useful at the counter to settle questions.

items: Keep earn and redeem in a ratio of ~5% discount (1 point per dollar, 20 points per dollar off). Anything more generous eats margin; anything less feels like an insult.
Use birthday bonuses — they're the single highest-converting loyalty mechanic.
Watch the Expired total. If it's a big number your clients aren't visiting often enough — shorten the expiry window or add a reminder campaign.

follow-up-tasks.title: Follow-up Tasks
follow-up-tasks.tagline: Automated callbacks, win-back, birthday, and no-show tasks. CRM-lite so no client slips through the cracks.
follow-up-tasks.roles: Add-ons permission to configure rules. Any team member with the Follow-ups permission works tasks.

blocks.text: Follow-up Tasks is a light CRM layer. It auto-generates tasks for the team — "Call Sarah about her colour two weeks after the service", "Win back Ahmed, no visit for 60 days", "Send birthday message to Maya" — and tracks outcomes so you know which conversations turned into revenue.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Follow-up Tasks → Activate.
Open Add-ons → Follow-up Tasks. The List, Today, and Kanban views sit at the top.
Set up auto-rules under Settings before relying on the system.

items.v: Every task with filters by status, type, member, priority, outcome, date. Bulk-complete, reassign, or snooze multiple tasks.
Three columns — Overdue, Due Today, Upcoming. The daily work view.
Columns by outcome state: Not Started, In Progress, Reached, Voicemail, No-Answer, Rebooked, Converted. Drag tasks between.

items: Click New Task.
Fill in: title, type (Callback / Reminder / Post-Treatment / Custom), client, assigned team member, due date, priority, notes.
Save. The task appears in the list and in Today if due today.

blocks.text: The real power is automation. Settings → Auto-Create Rules.

items: Turn on Auto-Create on Completion.
Add a rule: pick a service or category, choose task type, set how many days after completion to create, who to assign (the provider who delivered the service is a strong default).
Save. Every matching completed appointment now generates a follow-up task automatically.

blocks.text: When the team works a task, they record an outcome: Reached, Voicemail, No-Answer, Rebooked, Converted (linked to a sale). Outcomes drive retries.

items.v: Task closes.
If a retry rule matches, a new task is auto-created after the configured delay, up to a max retry count.
Client hasn't visited in N days (default 60). A win-back task auto-generates, assigned to the last provider.
A task is auto-created N days before each client's birthday.
Every missed appointment spawns a no-show follow-up so the team can check in and offer a reschedule.

blocks.text: Open any task and hit Send SMS, WhatsApp, or Email. A template textarea supports {firstName}, {lastName}, {fullName}, {businessName} variables. Sent messages are attached to the task's history.
When the message leads to a sale, hit Link Sale — the revenue attributes to the team member on the task, which feeds into reports.

items: Start with two rules — post-treatment callback at day 14 and lapsed client at 60 days. Add more as you see the pattern in your reports.
Always set an outcome. A task with no outcome is noise; a task with an outcome is data.
Link sales from converted tasks. The Staff Report shows the revenue every team member drove from follow-ups — the single clearest incentive to work the list.

client-attendance.title: Client Attendance
client-attendance.tagline: Walk-in queue and arrival tracking. Check clients in, assign to staff, hand off to checkout, and show a waiting-list TV in the lobby.
client-attendance.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Reception uses the queue with standard Client and Sales permissions.

blocks.text: Client Attendance is the queue you'd normally run on a whiteboard. A client walks in or arrives for an appointment — you check them in, the team sees them move through Waiting → Serving → Completed, and at the end of the visit the Complete & Checkout button takes you straight to the POS with everything pre-filled.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Client Attendance → Activate.
Open Add-ons → Client Attendance. The landing page is a live three-column queue.
Optionally configure Settings — walk-ins toggle, end-of-day auto-reset, staff notifications, and TV display per location.
Click + Check In.
Search the client by name or phone, or enter new-client details if walk-ins are allowed.
If they have an appointment today, the service auto-fills.
Confirm. They land at the back of the Waiting column with a queue number and a wait timer ticking.

blocks.text: Three stages, three actions.

items.v: Pick the team member and hit Move to Serving. The card jumps columns; the wait timer stops.
At the end of service, hit Complete & Checkout. The POS slideover opens pre-filled with the client and the service.
Remove the entry without checking out — useful if the client leaves the queue before being served.

blocks.text: The dashboard auto-refreshes every 30 seconds, so any team member looking at it sees new arrivals appear.

Every check-in over the last 30 days is in the History tab. Filter by date, status, assigned member. Useful for wait-time trends and for spotting patterns — busy hours, the day the lunchtime queue exploded, etc.

Generate a shareable URL per location that shows the waiting queue on a lobby TV. Clients see their queue number and an estimated wait. Nothing sensitive is exposed — just first names and queue positions.

items: Settings → scroll to TV Display.
Generate Link next to a location.
Open the URL on a Chromecast, Apple TV, or any browser pointed at the screen.
Regenerate Link if you ever need to revoke access.
Turn Enable Walk-Ins off if every visit must be pre-booked — some clinics run that way.
Keep the TV display on from open to close. Clients appreciate knowing the queue is moving.
Review History weekly to spot wait-time spikes and adjust staffing.

prescriptions.title: Prescriptions
prescriptions.tagline: Write digital prescriptions from a searchable drug catalog, with dosage templates, allergy checks, status tracking, and branded PDF output.
prescriptions.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Only team members with a prescriber title (Doctor, etc.) can finalise and sign prescriptions.

blocks.text: Prescriptions is the medication side of clinical work. Build a drug catalog once, reuse dosage templates forever, and write a prescription in a minute. Allergy conflicts flag at write time; status moves from Draft → Issued → Dispensed as the script makes its way to the patient and the pharmacy.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Prescriptions → Activate.
Open Settings — set your clinic license number, prescription header/footer, default language, and toggles (show generic names, show diagnosis).

blocks.text: Prescriptions → Drugs. The drug catalog is searchable and used throughout the add-on.

items: Add a drug: name, strength, form (tablet, syrup, injection), route (oral, IV), notes.
Edit anything; deactivate (not delete) drugs you no longer prescribe — historical prescriptions need them.
Import via CSV for bulk onboarding — the template is on the page.

blocks.text: Dosage templates are reusable "drug + dose + frequency + duration + quantity" snippets. Prescriptions → Templates.

items: Create a template — pick a drug, set dose ("500mg"), frequency ("3x daily"), duration ("7 days"), quantity.
At write-time, add a template row with one click instead of typing the same line for the 400th time.
Prescriptions → Create.
Pick the client and the prescriber.
Add medications — either from scratch or by dropping in a template.
If the client has recorded allergies, a warning banner appears at the top if any added drug clashes.
Save as Draft to keep editing, or Issue to finalise and generate the PDF.

items.v: Editable, not printable yet.
Finalised, printable, visible on the client portal.
Pharmacy has confirmed dispensing (manual marker for record-keeping).
Revoked — status badge shown on the PDF too.

blocks.text: From an Issued prescription, hit Print for the pad layout or Download PDF for the branded letterhead version. The same prescription is auto-visible to the patient on their client portal — no extra work.

items: Build templates for your top 20 prescribing patterns. 95% of your scripts will come from those 20.
Record patient allergies on the client profile. The allergy check is only as good as that record.
Keep the license number accurate — most regulators require it on every printed prescription.

lab-orders.title: Lab Orders
lab-orders.tagline: Order lab tests, receive and enter results, flag abnormal values, share with patients through the portal.
lab-orders.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Any clinician with the Lab Orders permission can create orders and enter results.

blocks.text: Lab Orders covers the whole lab workflow: order a test, send it to the lab, receive the result back (CSV, PDF, or manually entered), flag abnormal values, and optionally publish to the patient's portal.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Lab Orders → Activate.
Open Settings to set lab report header text and default currency.
Build the test catalog before creating any orders.

blocks.text: Lab Orders → Catalog. Organise tests into categories (Blood Work, Microbiology, Imaging) and define each test.

items: Test name, code, unit, normal range (min and max), price.
Categories can be reordered; tests can be moved between them.
Deactivate tests you no longer run but need for historical orders.
Lab Orders → New Order.
Pick the client and the ordering clinician.
Add tests from the catalog — each shows its reference range so you know what's abnormal once the result comes.
Save. The order shows up on the main list with status Ordered.
Open the order.
For each test, enter the value; if it's outside the normal range, flag it High, Low, or Abnormal.
Attach the lab's PDF or image as supporting evidence.
Save. Status moves to Results Received; a clinician review step is optional.

blocks.title: Alerts on abnormal values
blocks.body: Flagged values get a visual alert on the order detail and — if the Notifications add-on is enabled — can trigger a message to the ordering clinician.

items.v: Created but not yet sent to the lab.
Actively at the lab.
Values entered; flags applied.
A clinician has reviewed the results.
Order withdrawn.

items: Accurate normal ranges are the whole point. A missing or wrong range means abnormal flags are meaningless.
Attach the original lab PDF every time. Entered values are for querying; PDFs are the legal record.
Publish results to the patient portal only after review. The Reviewed status is what unlocks visibility.

medical-records.title: Medical Records
medical-records.tagline: SOAP notes with templates, vitals tracking, AI-suggested diagnoses, ICD-10 codes, and a finalised PDF for each visit.
medical-records.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Clinicians with the Medical Records permission write and finalise notes.

blocks.text: Medical Records holds the visit notes for each client. Templates enforce structure — chief complaint, examination, assessment, plan. Vitals are captured per visit and trended across history. If AI Assistant is on, the add-on suggests a diagnosis based on chief complaint and examination.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Medical Records → Activate.
Build at least one template before writing records — the structure pays off fast.

blocks.text: Templates are the skeleton of a record — which sections appear, which vitals are mandatory. Medical Records → Templates → Add Template.

items: Name the template ("General Checkup", "Post-op Follow-up").
Enable the sections you want: Chief Complaint, Examination, Assessment, Plan, custom sections.
Mark which vitals are required — BP, HR, temperature, weight, height.
Save. The template shows up in the record-creation dropdown.
Medical Records → Create.
Pick the client and, optionally, a template — required fields pre-appear.
Fill in the sections and the vitals block.
Add a diagnosis (free-text) and an ICD-10 code if you use them.
Set a follow-up date if the visit needs one.
Save as Draft while you iterate, or Finalise to lock and print.

blocks.text: Each finalised record contributes a vitals snapshot. The client profile has a Vital Trends chart — BP, HR, temperature, weight — across every visit. Useful for chronic-condition monitoring or just a weight-loss journey.

If the AI Assistant add-on is on, Draft records get a Suggest Diagnosis button. The AI reads the chief complaint and examination and returns a ranked list of likely diagnoses with reasoning — meant as a safety net, not a replacement for the clinician's judgement.
Only finalised records are printable. The PDF shows patient info, all sections, the vitals snapshot, and the ICD-10 code if present. Finalised records are also visible on the client portal.

blocks.title: Finalise carefully
blocks.body: Once finalised, a record can be reverted back to draft but an audit entry is kept. Don't finalise half-written drafts.

items: Build templates for your top three visit types. They'll cover 80 percent of your records.
Record vitals even when they feel unnecessary — they're the backbone of the trend chart.
Use the AI diagnosis as a checklist, not an oracle. Treat it like a second opinion from a junior.

clinical-imaging.title: Clinical Imaging
clinical-imaging.tagline: Upload X-rays, CBCT scans, clinical photos, and documents. Annotate, pair before/after, analyze with AI, share with the patient.
clinical-imaging.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Clinicians with Clinical Imaging permission upload and annotate; patients see images on the portal.

blocks.text: Clinical Imaging is the gallery for everything visual — periapical X-rays, panoramic, bitewing, CBCT, cephalometric, intra- and extra-oral clinical photos, smile and before-after photos, progress photos, digital scans, and PDF documents. Each image has a type, an optional tooth reference, annotations, and optional before/after pairing.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Clinical Imaging → Activate.
In Settings configure storage (S3 or local path), image-type labels, thumbnail quality, and any watermark for portal-shared images.
Open the client's gallery from Clinical Imaging → search the client.
Click Upload. Pick the file(s), select the image type, optionally enter a tooth number.
Save. Thumbnails generate automatically; the full image loads on demand.

blocks.text: Click any image to open the viewer. The annotation toolbar includes arrows, circles, rectangles, text labels, measurement lines, and freehand drawings. Annotations save with the image — non-destructive, always re-editable, never burned into the original file.

Pair two images (pre- and post-treatment) for a side-by-side view on both the clinician's screen and, with the patient's consent, the client portal. Useful for orthodontics, whitening, cosmetic dentistry, aesthetic procedures.

items: Open an image, click Pair with, and pick the other image.
The two are now linked. Either opens the side-by-side comparison.

blocks.text: If AI Assistant is enabled with a vision model, images get an Analyze button. The AI returns observations — possible caries on a periapical, asymmetry on a clinical photo — as a draft. A clinician has to confirm or dismiss before the finding becomes part of the record.

Patients see their images on the client portal's Images section. Before/after pairs only appear if the patient has consented to share them. Portal-visible images can optionally be watermarked — useful when sharing cosmetic results.

items: Pick S3 from day one if you can. Local storage is simple until the first gigabyte of scans shows up.
Use image types consistently. "Panoramic" vs "Panoramic X-ray" fragments your gallery and breaks filters.
Always tooth-number dental images. The dental chart and medical record pull these references for context.

dental-chart.title: Dental Chart
dental-chart.tagline: Interactive odontogram. Record conditions per tooth, per surface, across the whole arch; switch between adult and deciduous dentition.
dental-chart.roles: Add-ons permission to configure. Clinicians with Dental Chart permission update tooth conditions.

blocks.text: The Dental Chart is an interactive odontogram. Click any tooth, and a detail panel lets you mark conditions on each of its five surfaces (mesial, distal, occlusal, buccal, lingual). Conditions render as colour blocks on the chart, with a legend and a running summary underneath.

items: Dashboard → Add-ons → Dental Chart → Activate.
Settings → define the condition types you track (caries, filling, crown, missing, implant, root canal, bridge, veneer, sealant). Each has a colour and an FDI code.

blocks.text: Every client's chart can switch between adult (permanent) and deciduous (baby) dentition. The add-on uses FDI numbering — 1–32 for adult, 51–85 for primary — and renders the right tooth count automatically.

blocks.title: Children
blocks.body: For pediatric patients mid-transition, flip between the two views as needed — they're per-chart, not per-session.

items: Search for the client. The chart loads with any existing conditions painted in.
Click a tooth — the detail modal opens with its five surfaces.
Pick a condition per affected surface, add notes if needed, save.
The chart repaints instantly with the new colours.

blocks.text: Below the chart, a stats panel shows the totals: healthy teeth, affected teeth, per-condition counts. The legend maps colour to condition so the chart reads at a glance.

Print the chart for a case folder or a referral letter. The PDF includes the patient name, the date, the chart, the legend, and the statistics panel. Attach it to a medical record for a complete visit document.

items: Record per surface, not per tooth. A filling on the occlusal says something very different from a crown — the surface is the clinical detail.
Use the notes field — it's the difference between "caries on 36" and "caries on 36, distal, developing under existing filling".
Pair the chart with clinical imaging. A tooth reference on an X-ray and a matching colour on the chart is a tight clinical loop.
